result.markdown:
# Affordability Gaps {#sec-gaps} ## What sets the standard: Area Median Income ::: {#tbl-ami .cell layout-align="left" tbl-cap='Fauquier\'s income limits are set by the high-cost Washington, D.C. metro area'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Household size
Income limit 1-person 2-person 3-person 4-person 5-person 6-person 7-person 8-person
30% AMI 34900 39900 44900 49850 53850 57850 61850 65850
50% AMI 58150 66450 74750 83050 89700 96350 103000 109650
80% AMI 74800 85450 96150 106800 115350 123900 132450 141000
100% AMI 116250 132900 149500 166100 179400 192700 205950 219250
120% AMI 139500 159450 179350 199300 215250 231200 247150 263100
**Source:** HUD Section 8 Income Limits, FY2026; 100/120% AMI derived from published MFI. Limits for the Washington-Arlington-Alexandria, DC-VA-MD HUD Metro FMR Area (published median family income $166,100). DC-metro 80% limit is HUD-capped to the US median; 100/120% derived from published MFI.
````` ::: ::: - Fauquier is part of the **Washington, D.C. metro income-limit area**, whose published median family income is **$166,100** — far above what local wages (@fig-wages-costs) would suggest. - The **80% limit sits just below the 50% limit's usual spacing** because HUD caps the 80% figure to the national median; the large jump from 80% to 100% AMI is an artifact of that cap, not a data error. - For a **4-person household**, 80% AMI is $106,800 and 100% AMI is $166,100 — the reference points behind the affordability thresholds used throughout this chapter. ## What each income band can afford ::: {#tbl-afford-band .cell layout-align="left" tbl-cap='Even a middle-income household is priced well below the county\'s median home'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Band Household income Max affordable rent Max affordable purchase price
30% AMI $44,900 $1,122 $49,983
50% AMI $74,750 $1,869 $149,261
80% AMI $96,150 $2,404 $220,435
100% AMI $149,500 $3,738 $397,871
120% AMI $179,350 $4,484 $497,149
**Source:** HDAdvisors affordability calculation. Rent affordability at 30% of gross income. Purchase price assumes a 6.49% 30-year fixed rate, 10% down, a 28% front-end ratio, and $697/month taxes and insurance, for a 3-person household.
````` ::: ::: - A **50% AMI household can afford about $1,869 in rent** — below the county's median asking rent of $2,450 — and a home of roughly **$149,261**, a fraction of the $650,000 median sale price. - Affordability rises steeply with income: only at **100–120% AMI** does the affordable purchase price clear a quarter-million dollars, and even 120% AMI ($497,149) stops short of the median home. - These thresholds — not local wages — define who the market can and cannot serve; the gaps that follow measure the distance between them. ## Who bears the cost: households by income and tenure ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Renters cluster at the lowest incomes; owners dominate the top bands](gaps_files/figure-html/fig-hh-band-tenure-1.png){#fig-hh-band-tenure fig-align='left' fig-alt='Stacked bar chart of households by AMI income band and tenure for Fauquier County, Warrenton, and Bealeton. In the county, renters make up a large share of households in the two lowest income bands but a small share above 80% AMI, where owners dominate. Warrenton has a higher renter share across bands; Bealeton\'s town cells are small and footnoted for reliability.' width=100%} ::: ::: - **Low-income households skew heavily toward renting:** in the county's **≤30% and 30-50% AMI** bands renters are a large share of all households, while above **80% AMI** owners dominate — the tenure divide is an income divide. - **Warrenton carries a higher renter share across every band**, consistent with its role as the county's principal rental submarket; **Bealeton's** cells are small and are read as counts, not precise rates. - CHAS detail sums to roughly **25,900 occupied households** county-wide, the denominator behind the burden and gap figures that follow. ## Cost burden over time (ACS) ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Cost burden has eased since 2013, but renters remain far more burdened](gaps_files/figure-html/fig-burden-trend-1.png){#fig-burden-trend fig-align='left' fig-alt='Line chart of the share of cost-burdened households in Fauquier County from 2013 to 2024, split by tenure, with 2024 town points. The renter burden line declines gently from about 44% to 40%, staying well above the owner line, which falls from about 29% to 22%. In 2024, Warrenton and Bealeton renter points sit above the county renter line at roughly 55% and 59%.' width=100%} ::: ::: - **Renters are consistently the more burdened group:** about **40% of renters** were cost-burdened in 2024 versus **21% of owners** — a gap of nearly 20 points that has held across the decade. - Burden has **edged down, not up**, since 2013 (renters 44% → 40%; owners 29% → 21%), reflecting income growth and refinancing during a low-rate decade — but the levels remain high and the recent rate environment (@sec-market-ownership) points the other way. - **Both towns run hotter than the county on renter burden:** Warrenton (~55%) and Bealeton (~59%) 2024 renter points sit well above the county line, with Bealeton's cell footnoted for reliability. ## The core picture: burden is concentrated at the lowest incomes (CHAS) ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Cost burden is overwhelmingly a low-income problem](gaps_files/figure-html/fig-burden-band-1.png){#fig-burden-band fig-align='left' fig-alt='Stacked bar chart of cost burden by AMI income band and tenure for Fauquier County, faceted into renter and owner panels. At 30% AMI and below, the large majority of both renters and owners are cost-burdened, most of them severely; the burdened share falls sharply as income rises, and above 80% AMI almost no households are burdened. Renters are more burdened than owners within every band.' width=100%} ::: ::: - **Burden collapses onto the bottom of the income ladder:** at **≤30% AMI** the vast majority of both renters and owners are cost-burdened — most of them *severely* — while above **80% AMI** burden all but disappears. - **Renters are more burdened than owners within every band**, and severe burden is especially concentrated among the lowest-income renters, the households with the least room to absorb a rent increase. - Measured on the CHAS universe, county burden is **33% for renters and 20% for owners** — lower than the ACS headline (@fig-burden-trend) because CHAS uses a different denominator; the two are reconciled in the Data & Methodology appendix. ## The rental gap: too few homes for the lowest incomes ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![The county is short about 625 rentals affordable to its lowest-income renters](gaps_files/figure-html/fig-rental-gap-1.png){#fig-rental-gap fig-align='left' fig-alt='Diverging horizontal bar chart of the surplus or deficit of affordable and available rental units by AMI band in Fauquier County. Every band shows a deficit. The largest single-band shortfall relative to renter households is at 30% AMI and below (about negative 625 units); the deficit widens further in aggregate above 80% AMI.' width=100%} ::: ::: - The **deepest shortfall is at the very bottom:** the county has about **625 fewer rentals affordable *and available* to ≤30% AMI renters** than there are such renter households — the households with essentially no market alternative. - **Every band runs a deficit**, for a net shortfall of roughly **3,315 units** — higher-income renters occupy units that would otherwise be affordable to lower-income households, a competition-down-the-ladder dynamic. - The gap is a supply problem, not a targeting problem: even where units are nominally affordable, they are already occupied, leaving the lowest-income renters with the fewest options. ## The ownership gap: almost nothing for sale below 100% AMI ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Only 8 of 218 active listings are affordable below 100% AMI](gaps_files/figure-html/fig-ownership-gap-1.png){#fig-ownership-gap fig-align='left' fig-alt='Bar chart comparing, for each AMI band, the number of active for-sale listings affordable in that price range against the number of owner households in the band, Fauquier County. Listings affordable below 100% AMI are almost nonexistent (0, 0, 1, and 7 across the four lower bands), while 210 of the 218 listings fall only in the 120% AMI price range at the top.' width=100%} ::: ::: - Below the 100% AMI price ceiling there is **almost nothing for sale:** just **8 of 218 active listings** (0, 0, 1, and 7 across the 30%, 50%, 80%, and 100% AMI bands) — the for-sale market effectively excludes moderate-income buyers. - The overwhelming majority — **210 of 218 listings** — are priced only within reach of the **120% AMI** band or above, mirroring the median-price gap in @tbl-afford-band. - Owner-household counts far exceed affordable listings in every band below the top, so first-time and lower-income buyers face a market with essentially no entry point. ## The bottom line: wages don't reach the cost of housing ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Most local jobs don't pay enough to rent, let alone buy](gaps_files/figure-html/fig-wages-costs-1.png){#fig-wages-costs fig-align='left' fig-alt='Lollipop chart of average annual wages for major Fauquier employment sectors, shown for a single earner and a two-earner household, against two reference lines: the income needed to afford the median rent (about $98,000) and the income needed to buy the median home (about $190,586). A single all-jobs wage of $68,000 falls short of the rent line; only a two-earner all-jobs household ($136,000) clears the rent line, and no configuration reaches the purchase line. Retail wages are far below both.' width=100%} ::: ::: - **A single average local wage doesn't cover the rent:** all-jobs average pay is about **$68,000**, short of the **$98,000** needed to afford the median rent — and sector wages like Retail Trade ($41,934) fall far further behind. - **Only a two-earner household clears the rent bar:** two average local jobs ($136,000) exceed the $98,000 rent threshold — but **no configuration reaches the $190,586 needed to buy** the median home. - The message is structural: the county's wage base and its housing costs have decoupled, so even dual-income working households are priced out of ownership and the lowest-wage sectors are priced out of the market entirely. ## Town spotlight & interview validation ::: {.callout-note} ## Town spotlight — Warrenton and Bealeton carry the county's highest renter burden Both towns run **hotter than the county on renter cost burden**: in 2024 roughly **55% of Warrenton renters and 59% of Bealeton renters** were cost-burdened (@fig-burden-trend), versus 40% countywide. Warrenton's cell is High reliability; Bealeton's is Medium (CV 15–30%) and is read as a count-based signal, not a precise rate. As the county's principal rental submarket (@fig-hh-band-tenure), Warrenton concentrates both the renters and the burden — the same submarket where the affordable-rental deficit (@fig-rental-gap) bites hardest. ::: ## What the interviews said vs. what the data shows ::: {.callout-important} ## Interview validation - **"Workers can't afford to both work and live here."** *Confirmed.* A single average local wage ($68,000) falls short of the **$98,000** needed to afford the median rent, and even a two-earner household ($136,000) cannot reach the **$190,586** needed to buy the median home (@fig-wages-costs). Lower-wage sectors — Retail Trade at $41,934, Local Government at $58,079 — fall further behind, and the for-sale market offers **8 of 218** listings below 100% AMI (@fig-ownership-gap). The wage-to-housing-cost gap is not a perception; it is the defining structural feature of Fauquier's housing market. :::
